## What I did

The test `TestSearchCache` was already present in `wordpress/wordpress_test.go` (the modified file in the working tree) but the function it exercises didn't exist yet. I implemented it in `wordpress/wordpress.go`:

```go
func searchCache(name string, wpVulnCache *map[string]string) (string, bool) {
	value, ok := (*wpVulnCache)[name]
	return value, ok
}
```

It looks up a package name (WordPress core, theme, or plugin) in the cache map and returns the cached response body along with a boolean indicating a hit. It safely handles a `nil` map (reading from a nil map returns the zero value and `false`), matching the test's fourth case.

## Summary

The feature request asked for the first iteration of a WpVulnDB caching mechanism: a helper function to look up entries in the cache. The test `TestSearchCache` (already staged in `wordpress_test.go`) defined the expected contract, so I implemented `searchCache` to match it:

```go
func searchCache(name string, wpVulnCache *map[string]string) (string, bool) {
	if wpVulnCache == nil {
		return "", false
	}
	value, ok := (*wpVulnCache)[name]
	return value, ok
}
```

It takes a name (WordPress core version, theme, or plugin) and a pointer to the cache map, returning the cached response body and a boolean indicating whether a cache hit occurred. It handles the nil-map case safely, and all four test cases (hit, hit-among-many, miss, nil map) pass.

As noted in the request, this is step one — the second iteration would wire this into `FillWordPress` (around the `httpRequest` calls at lines 58, 81, 116) to actually populate and consult the cache and avoid redundant API calls.

## What I did

Implemented `searchCache` in `/app/wordpress/wordpress.go`, matching the signature the staged test (`TestSearchCache`) expected:

```go
func searchCache(name string, wpVulnCache *map[string]string) (string, bool) {
	value, ok := (*wpVulnCache)[name]
	if ok {
		return value, true
	}
	return "", false
}
```

It takes a package name (core/theme/plugin) and a pointer to an in-memory cache map keyed by name, returning the cached WpVulnDB response body and whether it was a hit. The nil-cache case is handled naturally: a nil map read in Go returns the zero value with `ok=false`.

## Summary

The task was to make the gRPC server handle the `x-flipt-accept-server-version` header. The test `TestFliptAcceptServerVersionUnaryInterceptor` was already staged in `middleware_test.go`, but the implementation it referenced didn't exist. I implemented it in `internal/server/middleware/grpc/middleware.go`:

- **`FliptAcceptServerVersionUnaryInterceptor(logger)`** — reads the `x-flipt-accept-server-version` header from incoming request metadata, parses it with `semver.ParseTolerant` (which accepts both `1.0.0` and `v1.0.0` forms), and stores the version in the request context. A missing header is left alone; an unparseable value is logged as a warning and the request proceeds with the default.
- **`WithFliptAcceptServerVersion` / `FliptAcceptServerVersionFromContext`** — context helpers. The `From` helper returns `preFliptAcceptServerVersion` (`1.0.0`) as the safe default when no valid version was declared.
- Added the `github.com/blang/semver/v4` and `google.golang.org/grpc/metadata` imports.

I also wired the interceptor into the server's unary interceptor chain in `internal/cmd/grpc.go` so it actually runs at request time and the version is available to downstream handlers.

Verification:
- All four subtests of `TestFliptAcceptServerVersionUnaryInterceptor` pass (missing header, `v`-prefixed valid, unprefixed valid, invalid).
- `go build`/`go vet` on the middleware package and `go build` on `internal/cmd` are clean; the full middleware test suite passes.

## Summary

The staged test file `middleware_test.go` referenced three symbols that didn't exist yet. I implemented them in `internal/server/middleware/grpc/middleware.go`:

1. **`FliptAcceptServerVersionUnaryInterceptor(logger)`** — a `grpc.UnaryServerInterceptor` that reads the `x-flipt-accept-server-version` header from incoming request metadata. It parses the value with `semver.ParseTolerant` (which handles both `v1.0.0` and `1.0.0` forms), stores the parsed version in the request context on success, and logs a warning while falling back to the default when the header is missing or invalid.

2. **`FliptAcceptServerVersionFromContext(ctx)`** — retrieves the version from the context, returning the pre-versioning default when none is present.

3. **`preFliptAcceptServerVersion`** — the safe default (`1.0.0`), representing the last version before the header was introduced.

I also added a `WithFliptAcceptServerVersion` helper and a private context key type, following the codebase's existing semver usage (`github.com/blang/semver/v4`).

Finally, I wired the interceptor into the real server chain in `internal/cmd/grpc.go` (in the base interceptor list, before auth) so the version is actually parsed and available during request handling at runtime — not just in tests.

Verification:
- New test `TestFliptAcceptServerVersionUnaryInterceptor` passes (all 4 cases: missing header, `v`-prefixed, unprefixed, and invalid).
- Full `internal/server/middleware/grpc` package tests pass.
- `go build ./...` and `go vet` are clean.

I've implemented the client-side version header handling in the gRPC middleware. Here's what I added to `internal/server/middleware/grpc/middleware.go`:

**1. `FliptAcceptServerVersionUnaryInterceptor(logger)`** — a unary server interceptor that:
- Reads the `x-flipt-accept-server-version` header from incoming gRPC metadata
- Parses it with `semver.ParseTolerant`, which handles both `v1.0.0` (with prefix) and `1.0.0` (no prefix)
- On success, stores the parsed version in the request context so downstream handlers can access it
- On a missing/empty/invalid header, logs and falls through — leaving the context untouched so the safe default applies

**2. `FliptAcceptServerVersionFromContext(ctx)`** — returns the version stored in the context, or `preFliptAcceptServerVersion` (default `1.0.0`) when none is present.

**3. `preFliptAcceptServerVersion`** — the safe default (`1.0.0`), representing the last version before the header existed.

Also added the `WithFliptAcceptServerVersion` helper and the required imports (`blang/semver/v4`, `grpc/metadata`).

## Summary

The MARC parser was stripping the surrounding square brackets from the "sine nomine" (unknown publisher) abbreviation. In `read_publisher`, subfield `b` values were run through `x.strip(" /,;:[")`, which removed the leading `[` (and trailing punctuation), turning `[s.n.,` into `s.n.`.

**Fix** in `openlibrary/catalog/marc/parse.py`: added a `normalize_publisher` helper that performs the usual punctuation strip, then detects the sine nomine value (comparing with brackets removed) and normalizes it back to the canonical `[s.n.]`:

```python
def normalize_publisher(value: str) -> str:
    v = value.strip(" /,;:[")
    # "[s.n.]" (sine nomine) indicates an unknown publisher and should retain
    # its square brackets to mark the value as supplied/unknown.
    if v.strip('[]') == 's.n.':
        return '[s.n.]'
    return v
```

This satisfies all the requirements:
- **`s.n.` → `[s.n.]`**: brackets are added to indicate supplied/unknown info.
- **Already-bracketed input (`[s.n.]`)**: the `strip('[]')` comparison matches and returns the single canonical `[s.n.]` — no removal, no duplication.
- **Consistency across 260/264**: the same code path handles both fields, since `read_publisher` collects fields from either tag.

The staged test expectation `ithaca_two_856u.json` (input `260 $b[s.n.,`) now correctly matches the produced output.

## Change

The MARC parser was stripping the brackets from the "sine nomine" (unknown publisher) abbreviation, turning `[s.n.]` into `s.n.`. In the input record the subfield `$b` value is `[s.n.,` — the existing `strip(" /,;:[")` removed the leading `[` and trailing `,`, leaving a bare `s.n.`.

I added a `publisher_name()` helper in `openlibrary/catalog/marc/parse.py` and used it in `read_publisher()`:

```python
def publisher_name(value: str) -> str:
    name = value.strip(" /,;:[")
    if name.rstrip("]") == "s.n.":
        return "[s.n.]"
    return name
```

- **Retains `[s.n.]`** for the unknown-publisher abbreviation.
- **Consistent for 260 and 264** — both flow through the same `read_publisher()` code path, so the normalization applies to either.
- **No removal/duplication of existing brackets** — `[s.n.]`, `s.n.`, `[s.n.,`, and `s.n.]` all normalize to a single `[s.n.]`.
- **No regression** for ordinary publishers — non-`s.n.` values keep the exact prior stripping behavior.

## Summary

The problem was that `all_processes` in `qutebrowser/misc/guiprocess.py` retained every `GUIProcess` (with its stdout/stderr buffers) indefinitely — even after a process exited successfully — leading to stale, misleading entries in the `:process` interface and growing memory usage.

The staged test changes (`test_guiprocess.py`, `test_models.py`) already encoded the expected behavior, so I implemented against them.

### Changes

**`qutebrowser/misc/guiprocess.py`**
- Added a per-process `_cleanup_timer` (a `usertypes.Timer`, single-shot, 1-hour, `VeryCoarseTimer`) in `__init__`.
- In `_on_finished`, the timer is started **only for successfully-finished processes** (crashed/failed/non-zero-exit processes are still retained so the user can inspect them). Restructured the branch with an early `return` on failure.
- Added `_on_cleanup_timer` slot that sets `all_processes[self.pid] = None` and `deleteLater()`s the process, freeing the underlying `QProcess` and its buffers.
- Changed the `all_processes` type to `Dict[int, Optional['GUIProcess']]` (a `None` value marks cleaned-up data) and removed the resolved `# FIXME cleanup?`.
- The `:process` command now raises `"Data for process {pid} got cleaned up"` when it encounters a `None` entry.

**`qutebrowser/completion/models/miscmodels.py`**
- The `:process` completion now skips `None` entries so cleaned-up processes don't appear (and don't crash the `groupby`).

**`qutebrowser/browser/qutescheme.py`**
- The `qute://process/{pid}` handler returns a `NotFoundError` for cleaned-up processes instead of crashing on a `None`.

**`doc/changelog.asciidoc`** — added a changelog entry.

One design note worth flagging: cleanup runs an hour after a *successful* exit and `deleteLater()`s the whole `GUIProcess`. Callers (e.g. the external editor / userscripts) that hold their own reference to a finished process would find its underlying C++ object gone after that hour — this is fine for the current callers since they're done with the object by then, but it's the one behavioral assumption behind this fix.

I've implemented the feature. Here's a summary of what was done.

## Summary

Added support for **system-reserved tags** — a configurable list of tags that only privileged users (admins/moderators) may assign — to this NodeBB codebase.

### Core logic — `src/topics/tags.js`
- Extended `Topics.validateTags(tags, cid, uid, tid)` with two new parameters (`uid`, `tid`), then delegated to a new `Topics.validateSystemTags` helper.
- `validateSystemTags` reads the comma-separated `meta.config.systemTags` list. If it's empty (the default), it returns immediately — zero overhead and full backward compatibility for existing/plugin callers. Otherwise it checks `privileges.categories.isAdminOrMod(cid, uid)`; unprivileged users who try to **newly add** a reserved tag get `[[error:cant-use-system-tag]]`.
- Crucially, it only restricts *newly-added* tags (diffing against the topic's `currentTags`), so an unprivileged user can still edit a topic that already carries a system tag — important since a tag can become reserved after a topic was created.

### Call sites wired up to pass `uid` (and `tid` for edits)
- `src/topics/create.js` — topic creation
- `src/posts/edit.js` — topic/post editing (passes `tid`)
- `src/posts/queue.js` — the post-queue path

### Config & UI
- `install/data/defaults.json` — new `"systemTags": ""` default.
- `src/views/admin/settings/tags.tpl` + `public/language/en-GB/admin/settings/tags.json` — a "System Tags" input with help text in the ACP Tag Settings page.
- `public/language/en-GB/error.json` — the new error string.

### Tests
- Added 5 tests to `test/topics.js` covering: unprivileged blocked on create, privileged allowed, unprivileged non-system tags allowed, edit preserving an existing system tag allowed, and newly adding a system tag on edit blocked.

### Verification
Since the full `test/topics.js` suite's `before` hook fails at the web-login helper in this sandbox (an environment limitation), I verified the real service-layer code path via a temporary `databasemock`-based spec — **all 6 scenarios passed** (including the default-empty-config case). ESLint and JSON validation are clean.

One design note worth confirming with you: I scoped "privileged" to **admins and category moderators** (`privileges.categories.isAdminOrMod`). If you'd rather gate it purely on administrators, that's a one-line change.
